issues.description: `lower_expr_iterative` is never called anywhere in the codebase (confirmed by grep). It is dead code. No test — unit or integration — can exercise it. A broken function silently ships with zero coverage.
issues.suggested_fix: Either add a call site (replacing or wrapping `lower_expr`) and add tests, or delete the function. At minimum add a `#[test] fn lower_expr_iterative_number_literal()` unit test calling it directly to establish a coverage baseline.

issues.description: For any `binary_expression` root, the code both (a) pushes children onto the stack for later iterative processing AND (b) immediately calls `self.lower_binary_expr(node)` which recursively lowers the full expression. Both the binary-expr result and each child's individually-lowered result are pushed to `results`. `results.last()` then returns the last-processed child (e.g., a number literal), not the root binary expression. A test asserting `lower_expr_iterative` on `"1 + 2"` produces a `BinOp` would fail — it would get a `Number` literal. No such test exists.
issues.suggested_fix: Add a test: parse `"1 + 2"` through `lower_expr_iterative` and assert the result is `ExprKind::BinOp { op: "+", .. }`. This will immediately expose the broken result-assembly. The fix requires a proper continuation-passing or two-stack approach — not `results.last()`.

issues.description: The `binary_expression` arm pushes children to the stack (claiming iterative processing) and then calls `self.lower_binary_expr(node)` as a "fallback" — which internally calls `self.lower_expr` recursively on those same children. For a deeply nested tree, this defeats the stack-overflow-avoidance goal entirely: recursion still occurs. The children are processed twice (once via the recursive call, once via the stack). No test checks depth behaviour or result correctness.
issues.suggested_fix: Add a test with a 200-level deep unary/binary nesting that would previously stack-overflow. It will either stack-overflow (proving the fix is ineffective) or return a wrong value (proving the result assembly is broken). Both are useful failure signals.

issues.description: The function's doc-comment states it exists to avoid stack overflow on deeply nested expressions, but there is no test that constructs a deeply nested expression (e.g., 100+ chained unary negations `-----...1`) and asserts it parses without panicking. Without this test, there is no way to verify the fix works or detect a regression back to the recursive path.
issues.suggested_fix: Add: `#[test] fn lower_expr_iterative_deep_nesting_no_overflow() { let src = format!("structure S {{ let x = {}1 }}", "-".repeat(200)); /* parse and assert Ok, no panic */ }`

issues.description: The `parenthesized_expression` arm uses `continue` to skip `results.push(expr)`, relying on the inner child being pushed to the stack separately. There is no test asserting that `lower_expr_iterative` on `(1 + 2)` produces the same result as on `1 + 2`. The semantics of silently dropping the parenthesized node from results is a subtle contract that needs a test.
issues.suggested_fix: Add a test comparing `lower_expr_iterative` output for `(42)` vs `42` — both should yield the same `Number` literal expression.

issues.description: The wildcard `_` arm calls `self.lower_expr(node)` recursively. This fallback re-introduces unbounded recursion for any node kind not explicitly handled (e.g., `range_expression`, `conditional_expression`, `lambda_expression`, `quantifier_expression`, `list_literal`, etc. — all of which exist in `lower_expr` but are absent from `lower_expr_iterative`'s match). No test verifies these node kinds are handled at all, let alone correctly.
issues.suggested_fix: Add tests for at least one node kind that falls through to the `_` arm (e.g., `range_expression`). Also consider whether those node kinds should be added to the explicit match arms.

issues.description: There is no property-based or parameterized test asserting that `lower_expr_iterative(node) == lower_expr(node)` for the same input. Without equivalence tests, any behavioral difference between the two functions goes undetected.
issues.suggested_fix: Add a table-driven test that runs a set of representative expressions through both `lower_expr` and `lower_expr_iterative` and asserts equal outputs.

sonnet_test_analyst.summary: The diff introduces `lower_expr_iterative` with zero tests and zero call sites — it is dead code that can never be exercised by the test suite. Beyond the coverage gap, the function contains a fundamental logical error: for any `binary_expression` node, it both pushes children onto the stack for iterative processing AND calls `lower_binary_expr` recursively on the same node, causing children to appear twice in `results`; the final `results.last()` then returns a child literal rather than the root expression. This means every non-trivial expression test would return the wrong AST node, but no such test exists to catch it. The `binary_expression` fallback to recursion also defeats the stated purpose of avoiding stack overflows. Three blocking issues are filed (dead code/no coverage, wrong return value for binary expressions, contradictory logic re-introducing recursion); four suggestions cover the missing deep-nesting stress test, the `parenthesized_expression` branch contract, the wildcard-fallback coverage gap, and the absence of equivalence tests between the two lowering functions.

issues.description: `lower_expr_iterative` is defined but never called anywhere in the codebase. A grep of all call sites confirms every consumer still calls `lower_expr`. The new function has zero effect — the stack-overflow risk it was created to address is entirely unmitigated.
issues.suggested_fix: Wire up `lower_expr_iterative` at the entry point(s) where deeply nested expressions are expected, or — if the function is not yet ready — do not land it on main until the integration is complete.

issues.description: The function returns `results.into_iter().last().flatten()` — the *last* element pushed to the results vec. Due to LIFO stack processing, for any compound expression the last element pushed is a leaf node, not the root. Example: `a + b` — the stack processes the binary node first (pushing its lowered result to `results`), then pops `b` (pushed second), then `a` (pushed last). `results.last()` is therefore `Some(Identifier(a))`, not the intended `Some(BinaryExpr(a,b))`. Every non-trivial expression silently produces a wrong AST node.
issues.suggested_fix: True iterative tree-lowering requires either (a) a two-phase approach (first pass pushes tasks, second pass assembles children into parents from a separate value stack), or (b) tracking the root's result index explicitly. The simplest correct design: push `(node, phase)` tuples; on first visit push children and re-push the node in 'assemble' phase; on assemble phase pop the required number of child results and build the parent.

issues.description: The stated goal is to avoid stack overflow via iterative processing, but the `binary_expression` arm pushes children onto the stack *and* immediately calls `self.lower_binary_expr(node)`, which recursively calls `self.lower_expr` for both children. The stack push is redundant dead work; the actual lowering still recurses. The same applies to `unary_expression` (→ `lower_unary_expr` → `lower_expr`), `function_call` (→ `lower_function_call` → `lower_expr`), and the `_` fallback which directly calls `self.lower_expr(node)`. Recursion depth is identical to the original implementation.
issues.suggested_fix: Remove the recursive delegation calls for the node kinds the function claims to handle iteratively. Binary and unary expressions must have their child results fed from the value stack rather than from recursive calls.

issues.description: `parenthesized_expression` uses `continue` to skip `results.push`, relying on the inner child being processed later and appearing as `results.last()`. This only works if the parenthesized expression is the root node AND there is exactly one inner expression that happens to be a leaf. For any deeper nesting (e.g., `((a + b))`), two `continue` skips fire before any result is pushed, and then the binary expression arm pushes both the BinaryExpr result *and* the two leaf results; `results.last()` is again a leaf, not the wrapped expression.
issues.suggested_fix: Parenthesized expressions should be treated as transparent pass-throughs in the iterative loop by replacing them with their inner node on the stack (i.e., `stack.push(inner_child)` without pushing the paren node itself to results).

issues.description: The child filter `child.is_named() && child.kind() != "(" && child.kind() != ")"` is redundant. In Tree-sitter, `(` and `)` are anonymous nodes and `is_named()` already excludes them. The `kind()` checks are dead conditions copied from the `lower_expr` fallback.
issues.suggested_fix: Remove the `child.kind() != "(" && child.kind() != ")"` guards; `child.is_named()` is sufficient.

sonnet_bug_hunter.summary: The new `lower_expr_iterative` function is broken in every significant way. It is never wired into the call graph (dead code), so the stated stack-overflow fix has no effect. The return value logic (`results.last()`) is fundamentally wrong — LIFO stack processing means the last result pushed is always a leaf node, not the root expression, causing silent wrong-AST production for any compound expression. Recursion is not actually eliminated: `binary_expression`, `unary_expression`, `function_call`, and the catch-all `_` arm all delegate to their existing recursive helpers unchanged. The `parenthesized_expression` `continue`-skip breaks the results ordering invariant except in the trivial single-leaf case. This function must not be called in its current state; it requires a correct two-stack (work-stack + value-stack) iterative pattern before it can replace `lower_expr`.

issues.description: `lower_expr_iterative` is never called anywhere in the codebase. The entire method is dead code. The stated goal — preventing stack overflow on deeply nested expressions — is not achieved because no call-site has been updated to use it.
issues.suggested_fix: Either wire it up by replacing the `lower_expr` call-site(s) that process user-provided (potentially deep) expressions, or remove the method entirely until the algorithm is correct.

issues.description: The result-collection strategy `results.into_iter().last().flatten()` is semantically wrong for a tree. For any non-leaf expression (e.g., `a + b`), the stack-based loop pushes the root first, processes it (pushing its result), then pushes and processes the children — so the last entry in `results` is a leaf node (e.g., `Ident("a")`), not the root. The method would return the wrong expression for any compound input.
issues.suggested_fix: A correct iterative tree lowering requires post-order evaluation: use a task enum (e.g., `enum Task { Lower(Node), Combine(NodeKind) }`) so that child results are combined into parent results after children are processed. Alternatively, use a continuation-passing style with a second 'build' stack.

issues.description: The `binary_expression` arm pushes the node's children onto the stack AND simultaneously calls `self.lower_binary_expr(node)`, which itself calls `self.lower_expr` recursively on both children. This (a) defeats the purpose of the iterative traversal for the one case that causes deep recursion, and (b) processes both the root and its children twice: once via the recursive call and once when the pushed children are later popped off the stack.
issues.suggested_fix: Remove the `self.lower_binary_expr(node)` fallback call from the `binary_expression` arm entirely. The correct approach is to defer construction of the `BinOp` node until after children have been lowered (see the combine-phase pattern described in the result-collection fix above).

issues.description: In the `parenthesized_expression` arm, the guard `child.kind() != "(" && child.kind() != ")"` is redundant. The outer check `child.is_named()` already excludes anonymous punctuation tokens like `(` and `)` — tree-sitter marks them as unnamed. The extra kind checks are dead filtering logic that implies punctuation nodes are named, which is misleading.
issues.suggested_fix: Remove the `child.kind() != "(" && child.kind() != ")"` guards; keep only `child.is_named()`. This matches how `lower_expr` itself handles the same case.

issues.description: `lower_expr_iterative` handles only 7 of the ~18 node kinds in `lower_expr` (`number_literal`, `string_literal`, `bool_literal`, `identifier`, `binary_expression`, `unary_expression`, `function_call`). All other kinds — `range_expression`, `conditional_expression`, `match_expression`, `lambda_expression`, `quantifier_expression`, `quantity_literal`, `list_literal`, `set_literal`, `map_literal`, `index_access`, `member_access`, etc. — fall through to `_ => self.lower_expr(node)`, re-entering the recursive path. The iterative variant would be ineffective for expressions containing these node types.
issues.suggested_fix: Either expand the match arms to cover all the same node kinds as `lower_expr`, or clearly document which expression types are intentionally left to the recursive fallback and why they cannot overflow in practice.

sonnet_design_critic.summary: The new `lower_expr_iterative` method has three blocking problems that together make it completely non-functional. First, it is dead code — no call-site was updated to use it, so it provides no stack-overflow protection today. Second, its result-collection logic (`results.into_iter().last()`) is algorithmically wrong for trees: children are pushed and processed after the root, so the last `results` entry is always a leaf rather than the root expression. Third, the `binary_expression` arm — the primary motivation for iterative traversal — immediately falls back to `self.lower_binary_expr(node)` (recursive), and then also pushes children that will be redundantly re-processed. A correct iterative tree lowering requires a post-order combine phase (e.g., a `Task::Combine` enum on a second stack) so that child results can be assembled into parent nodes after children are lowered; the current flat-`Vec`-of-results design cannot express that. Two additional suggestions: the `(` / `)` kind guards inside `parenthesized_expression` are redundant given the surrounding `is_named()` check, and roughly half the `lower_expr` node kinds fall through to the recursive fallback, undermining the feature's stated goal even if the algorithmic issues were fixed.
